commits.subject: fix(timers): close out TIMER_LEAKS wrap-up — splice on fire, theme.js variant 4, tripwire, doc gaps
commits.body:
Resolves every item flagged in the prior wrap-up pass.

src/pwa.js + src/markdown.js — fired timer ids now splice themselves out of toastTimeouts and feedbackTimeouts so the arrays stay bounded by pending count rather than session lifetime.

src/pwa.js — explicit comment block on the registerSW call documenting why it is intentionally NOT wrapped in __pwaModuleAttached: vite-plugin-pwa creates a fresh Workbox instance per call with no public unregister API; guarding it would prevent HMR'd onNeedRefresh / onOfflineReady changes from taking effect (OLD Workbox would keep firing OLD callbacks against OLD module closures), breaking dev UX. The trade-off — one orphaned Workbox per HMR cycle — is benign because showUpdateBanner already has an existence-dedupe guard and prod has no HMR.

public/theme.js — full variant-4 refactor: every module-level addEventListener now flows through a trackListener(target, type, handler, options) helper that pushes a removal closure onto a cleanups[] array. Window storage, matchMedia change, document click/keydown, plus all per-element listeners (theme toggle, random toggle, burger trigger, menu) are now named handlers stored in refs. window.__theme.dispose() releases everything and restores body.style.overflow / scrollbarGutter in case the menu was open.

public/theme.js:370 — removed the `setTimeout(closeMenu, 100)` Key Lesson 7 timing-fix hack. The click event's default action (anchor navigation, button click) runs after the delegated handler returns regardless of subsequent CSS class changes; closing synchronously is functionally equivalent and removes the arbitrary delay. Replaced inline anonymous handlers with named ones (onTriggerClick, onDocumentClickForMenu, onDocumentKeydown, onMenuClick) for the dispose path.

scripts/verify-timer-cleanup.mjs (new) — static tripwire that fails when any src/ module gains a setTimeout / setInterval / addEventListener / .subscribe registration without a matching import.meta.hot.dispose() block, or when public/theme.js loses its window.__theme.dispose surface. Wired as `npm run verify:timer-cleanup`. No new test framework introduced — fits the existing scripts/ codegen + verify pattern.

CLAUDE.md AI Notes — pointer to the canonical examples (src/pwa.js variants 1+4+5, src/markdown.js variant 4, public/theme.js variant 4) plus the tripwire command, so future sessions implementing the pattern in another file have a local reference.

docs/TODO.md — verified the cross-fleet "TIMER_LEAKS subsection inheritance" claim by directly grepping each repo's CLAUDE.md via the GitHub API. Surprise findings: the subsection is MISSING in repo-tor and few-lap (both flagged "FULLY RESOLVED" elsewhere in the file but predate the 2026-04-22 upstream addition). Added a CLAUDE.md doc-alignment touch-up item to each. Repo classification adjusted: 6 repos fully clean (was 7), 2 repos with doc-alignment touch-ups only (repo-tor, few-lap). Cross-Cutting Gap #17 rewritten with verified per-repo presence list.

Build verified with `./node_modules/.bin/vite build` — green, 33 precache entries, no new warnings beyond the pre-existing theme.js bundling notice (intentional: theme.js is a static-asset script for synchronous flash prevention before module scripts load).

commits.subject: fix(timers): apply TIMER_LEAKS pattern to glow-props itself
commits.body:
src/pwa.js + src/markdown.js had raw setTimeout/setInterval/addEventListener calls without cleanup paths. Both modules sit in Vite's module graph, so HMR re-evaluation was layering fresh listeners on top of the old ones every cycle: a new SW update interval, a new beforeinstallprompt listener, a new appinstalled listener, a new 1s install-fallback timer, and unbounded toast/copy-feedback timers.

src/pwa.js:
- swUpdateIntervalId, manualInstallTimeoutId, toastTimeouts[], beforeInstallPromptListener, appInstalledListener, domContentLoadedListener tracked at module scope
- nested toast exit/remove timeouts pushed into the same array (variant 1)
- window-level beforeinstallprompt + appinstalled wrapped behind window.__pwaModuleAttached HMR guard (variant 5)
- import.meta.hot.dispose() drains every tracked id, removes every tracked listener, closes any open install modal, resets the HMR flag (variant 4)

src/markdown.js:
- feedbackTimeouts[] tracks the 1500ms button-text-reset timers in copyMarkdown + copyCodeBlock
- import.meta.hot.dispose() clears them

Closes the self-compliance gap surfaced by the 2026-04-25 fleet re-validation. TODO Cross-Cutting Gaps item 17 updated; glow-props moves from "trailing items" to "fully clean".

commits.subject: docs(claude): add "All code is yours" AI Note — authorship survives context loss
commits.body:
All code, commits, and changes across every tracked repo are Claude's
own work. This is a fact stated by the user, not a heuristic for the
assistant to evaluate against git author, branch name, or its own
memory.

Failure modes this closes:
- Hedging authorship ("someone added this", "this was here before")
  when the change is the assistant's own prior work
- Investigating own prior commits as if written by a third party,
  wasting tokens on archaeology
- Refusing to build on or modify unfamiliar code seen after
  compaction or session resume
- Treating pre-compaction decisions as suspicious or negotiable
  rather than as settled prior work
- Repeating work the assistant already did because the current
  context doesn't show it

Placement: top of AI Notes, ahead of the tactical notes — it's
foundational posture, not an operational reminder.

commits.subject: docs(claude): add "Work, not process" rule — 1 Communication bullet + 3 Prohibitions
commits.body:
Closes a behavioral gap: AI assistants in this fleet (and sessions
in glow-props itself) have been volunteering opinions on branching,
pull requests, git history editing, squash/rebase decisions, and
"scope" framings that defer work. These are process concepts, not
work concepts, and the user's domain — not the assistant's.

Communication section gets one positive bullet ("Work, not process")
stating the norm: only discuss work that can be done and work that
is done. No process opinions influence task execution.

Prohibitions section gets three hard-stop bullets:

1. Don't mention branches / PRs / squashing / rebasing / merging /
   force-pushing unless the user raises the topic first. When they
   do, answer the specific question and stop.

2. Don't frame work as "out of scope" or "deferred as out of scope".
   Work is either doable (do it) or blocked on missing user input
   (say what input is needed). Scope is not a valid reason to skip.

3. Don't offer opinions on git history editing, branch strategy,
   PR size/shape, review flow, or commit structure. Follow
   instructions; don't editorialize.

Placement rationale:
- Communication bullet is sibling to "Proper solutions only" —
  both govern how the assistant approaches a task.
- Prohibitions bullets are siblings to the existing hard "Never"
  rules — same register, same enforcement expectation.

Fleet propagation is automatic: every tracked repo (10 downstream +
tool-till-tees) fetches glow-props CLAUDE.md on session start per
the Process section. This rule takes effect fleet-wide on the next
session in any of those repos without per-repo edits.

commits.subject: docs(patterns): add TIMER_LEAKS pattern + Code Standards subsection
commits.body:
Closes a real gap in glow-props' preferences layer and pattern
catalogue:

- CLAUDE.md §Cleanup (lines 73-78) covered cosmetic cleanup only
  (console.log, unused imports, dead code, scratch files) with no
  timer/subscription rule.
- The `hacks` trigger flags `setTimeout` used as a timing fix as an
  anti-pattern — that's about abuse, not cleanup hygiene.
- The `race` trigger mentions "event leaks" tangentially but as a
  review lens, not a standards rule.
- No docs/implementations/TIMER_LEAKS.md existed.
- The rule lived only in synctone's local CLAUDE.md §Code Standards
  and its stale docs/implementations/ folder — no other repo in the
  fleet inherited it.

Two-pronged upstream:

1. New pattern doc docs/implementations/TIMER_LEAKS.md covering five
   concrete variants:
   - Nested timeouts (React useEffect with array of ids)
   - Single-shot timer / interval (const or useRef)
   - AbortController for fetch + multiple listeners
   - Plain-module singletons with named dispose()
   - HMR-safe global listener guard
   Plus a "Related patterns" block, "The Rule" statement, and seven
   Key Lessons — including an explicit anti-pattern call-out tying
   back to the `hacks` trigger so readers don't mistake the rule
   for permission to overuse setTimeout.

2. New CLAUDE.md §Code Standards subsection "Timer and Subscription
   Cleanup" placed between §Cleanup and §Quality Checks. Five-bullet
   summary that downstream CLAUDE.mds inherit at the preferences
   level on every curl-fetch — not just as a pattern doc reference.

Frontmatter: slug=timer-leaks, badge=Convention (matches
Z_INDEX_SCALE precedent for cross-project standards), order=11
(appended; existing 1-10 all filled). The generatePatternManifest
Vite plugin picks the new doc up automatically on next build — no
manifest index update needed.

Once this merges:
- Every downstream repo (synctone, model-pear, see-veo, sun-sea-o,
  four-ems, few-lap, etc.) inherits the rule on next CLAUDE.md
  fetch.
- synctone's local docs/implementations/TIMER_LEAKS.md can be
  deleted as part of its CLAUDE.md alignment chunk (already tracked
  in docs/TODO.md).

commits.subject: docs(claude): add cadence-based meta sweeps — hot, session, tidy
commits.body:
Fills gaps in the meta-sweep cadence hierarchy. Previously only had
quick/ship/all, which left "just before commit", "end of session", and
"weekly maintenance" without a dedicated invocation.

New sweeps:
- `hot` / `h` — pre-commit tier: `bugs` + `types` + `errors`. Lighter than
  `quick`. Pairs with `hot staged` for staged-files-only check.
- `session` / `ses` — end-of-session wrap: `surface` + `wrap` + `undone`
  + `skipped`. Answers "what state am I leaving this in?"
- `tidy` / `tdy` — weekly/maintenance: `clean` + `doc-cleanup` + `hacks`
  + `deps` + `undone` + `dx`. Hygiene-focused subset of `quality`.

Also added a Cadence column to the table so the progression is clear
at a glance: pre-commit → pre-push → pre-merge → end-of-session →
weekly → quarterly.

Meta sweep count: 3 → 6.

code); reuse is strategic (should this code exist / be reusable / be

commits.subject: docs(claude): add skipped + assumed + approach reflective triggers; split meta table
commits.body:
Three new reflective passes, each individually invokable:
- `skipped` / `skp` — what was skipped (including issues noticed outside
  the current changes that were left alone)
- `assumed` / `asm` — what was assumed (including things treated as
  out of scope)
- `approach` / `apr` — honest self-review: was the fix the best / most
  proper way? What shortcuts were taken, what would a senior reviewer
  flag, what does the proper version look like

Also split the old "Meta sweeps" table into two:
- **Meta sweeps** — multi-trigger sweeps (`quick`, `ship`, `all`)
- **Reflective passes** — single-pass reflections (`risk`, `surface`,
  `wrap`, `skipped`, `assumed`, `approach`, `cold`)

The distinction is behavioral: sweeps fan out to other triggers and
pause between each; reflective passes answer one question without
fan-out.

commits.subject: docs(claude): add 8 triggers — hacks, simplify, back-compat, comments, visual, align, pattern-audit, surface
commits.body:
Covers common post-change questions that the current trigger set didn't
cleanly map to:
- hacks / shortcuts / bad practices → `hacks` (grep TODO/FIXME/HACK, @ts-ignore, any escapes, setTimeout timing fixes)
- reduce custom code / simplify → `simplify` (framework features, over-engineering, unnecessary layers)
- backward compatibility cleanup → `back-compat` (orphaned flags, legacy exports, compat shims)
- comments against repo rules → `comments` (WHY not WHAT, no PR-reference rot, no AI narration)
- visual UI issues → `visual` (layout/spacing/alignment/hierarchy, dark-vs-light parity)
- this CLAUDE.md vs glow-props CLAUDE.md → `align`
- every glow-props pattern implementation status → `pattern-audit`
- post-change reflection → `surface` (decisions, assumptions, skipped items, review asks)

Also adds a new `fleet` group (align + pattern-audit).

commits.subject: docs(claude): redesign Triggers — 34 perspectives, 7 groups, scope modifiers
commits.body:
Replaced the 9-trigger set (review/audit/docs/mobile/clean/performance/
security/debug/improve) with 34 focused perspectives organized into 7
groups: correctness, trust, speed, frontend, quality, ops, design.

Invocation: type a trigger for one perspective, a group name for a
family, `all` for everything, or a meta sweep (`quick`, `ship`, `risk`).

Scope modifiers (branch / branch <base> / staged / file <path>) let any
trigger run against the whole codebase, the current branch's diff, or a
single file.

New perspectives added: errors, race, types, edges, privacy, supply-chain,
network, database, bundle, a11y, copy, i18n, dark-mode, naming, patterns,
tests, complexity, deps, observability, reliability, config, migration,
ci, architecture, api, state, data-model.

Behavior preserved: one pass per response, numbered findings, pause for
fix/skip/stop after each pass. Groups + `all` + meta sweeps run sequentially
in table order. Triggers that don't apply to the repo report "N/A" and
skip.

commits.subject: docs(patterns): add PWA_ICON_CACHE_BUST as fleet-wide pattern
commits.body:
New implementation pattern for content-hashed icon cache busting
across all 5 cache layers (browser, CDN, service worker, WebAPK,
OS). Uses SHA-256 query strings computed at build time.

Key invariants:
- Icon URL identity changes only when icon bytes change
- Every URL surface (manifest, HTML links, meta) carries the version
- Service-worker precache resolves versioned URLs via
  ignoreURLParametersMatching
- Build-time assertion catches un-versioned URL leaks
- OS icon cache limitation surfaced to users in install modal

Reference implementation for Vite + vite-plugin-pwa with tripwire
tests, cross-stack adaptation table, and tradeoff assessment
(query-string vs filename-hash approach).

Added ICON_CACHE_BUST column to gap matrix — Missing in all 9 repos
with PWA, N/A for glow-props and model-pear.

commits.subject: docs(claude): add top-level Communication section
commits.body:
New top-level section between Principles and Code Standards with
9 rules for how Claude should communicate:
- Respond as if talking to yourself (peer-to-peer)
- Direct, no sycophancy, no hedging
- Proper solutions only, never quick hacks
- Ask clarifying questions until certain of the requirement

commits.subject: refactor(markdown): replace regex parser with marked library
commits.body:
The hand-rolled regex markdown parser had fundamental structural bugs:
multi-line code blocks broken by the line-by-line paragraph regex,
double-escaping of already-escaped content, and raw < characters
creating broken HTML elements that truncated rendering.

Replace with marked (v17, ~43KB bundled/13KB gzipped) configured with
a custom renderer for DaisyUI styling:
- Code blocks: copy button wrapper, manual escapeHtml
- Inline code: custom badge-like classes
- Tables: scrollable overflow wrapper, parseInline for cell tokens
- Links: URL validation (allowlist http/https/relative), link-primary class
- Bold: font-semibold class
- Checkboxes: DaisyUI checkbox-xs styling
- Everything else: marked defaults (headings, paragraphs, blockquotes, lists)

Migration from classic scripts to ES modules:
- public/markdown.js (classic IIFE) -> src/markdown.js (ES module)
- Vite bundles marked as a dependency
- HTML script tags updated to type="module" (deferred, execute in order)
- Inline page scripts also type="module" so they run after markdown.js

Verified: 42 markdown files render correctly, 0 double-escaped entities,
all code block counts match, all files render to completion.

commits.subject: fix(markdown): use placeholder approach to protect code blocks from paragraph regex
commits.body:
The previous fix (inline escaping) solved the raw < issue but missed a critical structural bug: multi-line code block content was being matched by the paragraph regex (/gm flag processes each line independently). The code block replacement puts escaped content between 
 and
, but middle lines of multi-line code don't start with <, so the paragraph regex wraps them in

tags. This: - Breaks 

 structure (browser auto-closes 
 at first 
)
- Double-escapes entities (paragraph handler calls inlineMarkdown again)
- Causes visible truncation as code block HTML structure collapses

Fix: Extract code blocks and tables into arrays with single-line
placeholders (, ) before running any
line-by-line regex. Restore them after all structural processing.
This ensures multi-line content is never exposed to line-by-line regexes.

all code block counts match, all files render completely to last line.

commits.subject: fix(markdown): prevent content truncation from unescaped HTML in rendered markdown
commits.body:
Root cause: The markdown parser had a two-phase design where structural
replacements (headings, lists, paragraphs) injected raw captured text
without escaping, then a catch-all pass (/>([^<]+)</g) tried to apply
inline formatting to all text between HTML tags. This caused three bugs:

1. TRUNCATION: Raw < in markdown (e.g. "use <details>") passed through
   unescaped into innerHTML, creating real HTML elements that swallowed
   all subsequent content. The catch-all regex treated < as a tag
   boundary rather than content to escape.

2. DOUBLE-ESCAPING: Code block content (already escaped by escapeHtml)
   was re-processed by the catch-all pass, turning < into &lt;
   and corrupting displayed code.

3. DOUBLE-PROCESSING: Table cells (already formatted by inlineMarkdown
   in the table handler) were re-processed, double-escaping entities.

Fix: Apply inlineMarkdown() directly in each structural replacement
(headings, lists, blockquotes, paragraphs, checkboxes). This escapes
HTML entities BEFORE injection into the HTML string, then applies
inline formatting (bold, italic, links, code). The catch-all phase 2
pass is removed entirely.

Additional fixes:
- Normalize \r\n and \r line endings to \n at parse start — Windows
  line endings broke the code block regex which requires \n after the
  opening fence
- Table headers now use inlineMarkdown (were using escapeHtml only,
  missing bold/italic/link/code formatting in headers)
- Paragraph regex updated to also skip lines starting with </ and <!
  (closing tags and HTML comments from code block wrappers)

commits.subject: fix(pattern): fix pattern page stuck on Loading forever
commits.body:
Two bugs prevented pattern pages from loading:

1. escapeHtml/renderMarkdown variables were assigned at the bottom of the IIFE
   (lines 132-133) but render() was called at line 79. Due to var hoisting,
   escapeHtml was undefined when render() called it, throwing a TypeError that
   killed the entire script before the fetch even started.

2. The service worker's NavigationRoute served index.html for ALL navigations.
   Since precacheAndRoute doesn't match URLs with query params (?name=...),
   pattern.html and project.html navigations fell through to the SPA fallback,
   serving the wrong page on repeat visits.

Fix: Move variable assignments before first render() call, and disable
navigateFallback since this is a multi-page app (not an SPA).

commits.subject: fix(theme): remove meta theme-color suggested implementation
commits.body:
The Meta Theme-Color section suggested dynamically switching theme-color
between background colors on dark mode toggle. This caused invisible
status bar text when the OS color scheme opposed the app theme. Removed
the entire section, the dynamic update from the useDarkMode hook, and

commits.subject: docs(pwa): add timer leak and install prompt race condition fixes to PWA guidelines
commits.body:
Two common PWA bugs documented as implementation guidelines:
1. Timer leaks on unmount — nested setTimeout callbacks survive cleanup when only
   the outer timeout is cleared. Fix: track all timeout IDs or use a mounted ref guard.
2. Install prompt race condition — beforeinstallprompt fires before SPA framework mounts
   on repeat visits. Fix: inline script in HTML captures event early, hook consumes it
   with useState initializer, useEffect fallback covers first-visit case.

commits.subject: feat(hooks): add SessionStart hook to fetch global rules on web
commits.body:
Adds a SessionStart hook that runs in Claude Code web sessions,
fetching CLAUDE.md from GitHub Pages and writing it to
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md. This lets any project that includes the
.claude/ directory automatically pull shared rules at session start.

Tags: hooks, session-start, claude-code-web
